sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 maze puzzle. Player is a white cross (W/w) at approximately (32, 21) inside a gray (G) maze structure. The maze has corridors and rooms. Key elements: charcoal L-shaped obstacle (node 6), orange rectangle (node 10) and blue rectangle (node 11) in the lower area, a black-framed blue L-shape at top (nodes 3-5), and another at bottom-left (nodes 12-14). The yellow bar at bottom is HUD. Goal likely involves navigating the maze to reach the orange/blue target area.
- Plan: Probe by moving RIGHT to see if the player moves through the maze.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The player didn't move - the white cross is still at the same position after RIGHT. But the board changed and gameplay changed. Let me check what actually changed - maybe something else moved or changed. Let me compare the before and after frames.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT action changed the board but the player (white cross) stayed at the same position. Need to check what actually changed - maybe something else moved or the HUD changed differently.
Plan: Compare before/after frames to see what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Compare before and after frames to see what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation['nodes']
after_seg = after.segmentation['nodes']

print("=== Before ===")
for n in before_seg:
    if n['color'] in ('W', 'w', 'O', 'b', 'B'):
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("=== After ===")
for n in after_seg:
    if n['color'] in ('W', 'w', 'O', 'b', 'B'):
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check the HUD area specifically
print()
print("=== HUD comparison (rows 60-63) ===")
before_lines = before.ascii.split('\n')
after_lines = after.ascii.split('\n')
for i in range(60, 64):
    b = before_lines[i] if i < len(before_lines) else ""
    a = after_lines[i] if i < len(after_lines) else ""
    if b != a:
        print(f"Row {i} CHANGED:")
        print(f"  Before: [{b}]")
        print(f"  After:  [{a}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see what happened! The RIGHT action moved the orange and blue rectangles to the right (from cols 34-38 to cols 39-43). The player didn't move. So the actions don't move the player - they move something else.
Looking at the change:
- Node 10 (orange): boundary changed from [[45, 34], [45, 38], [46, 38], [46, 34]] to [[45, 39], [45, 43], [46, 43], [46, 39]] - moved right by 5 columns
- Node 11 (blue): boundary changed from [[47, 34], [47, 38], [49, 38], [49, 34]] to [[47, 39], [47, 43], [49, 43], [49, 39]] - moved right by 5 columns
Also the HUD changed slightly (a G appeared in the yellow bar area).
So the direction keys move the orange/blue pair. The goal is probably to move them to some target location. Let me think about what the target could be.
Looking at the layout:
- The orange/blue pair is in the lower part of the gray maze
- There's a black-framed blue L-shape at the top (nodes 3-5) and one at bottom-left (nodes 12-14)
- The charcoal L-shape (node 6) is an obstacle
Maybe the goal is to move the orange/blue pair to match the blue L-shape pattern? Or to fit them into some space?
Let me try more movements to understand the mechanics better. Let me try LEFT to see if it reverses, and also try UP/DOWN.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT action moved the orange/blue pair rightward by 5 columns (from cols 34-38 to 39-43). The player (white cross) doesn't move - the directional keys control the orange/blue object pair. The goal likely involves navigating this pair to a target location within the maze.
Plan: Test more directions to understand movement mechanics and find the target. Try LEFT to reverse, then UP/DOWN.
